skill.name: webapp-testing
skill.description: Playwright-based guidance and helper scripts for testing local web applications, inspecting rendered UI, capturing screenshots, and collecting browser console logs.
skill.summary: Test local web applications with Playwright scripts, screenshots, DOM inspection, and console log capture.
skill.author: ComposioHQ

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most weak-crypto, hardcoded URL, and local file-write alerts are false positives from license text, localhost examples, and screenshot/log artifacts. The confirmed concern is the bundled server helper, which accepts command-line strings and starts server commands with shell=True, while the skill text discourages source inspection before running bundled scripts. No evidence found of malicious network exfiltration or credential harvesting, but the command execution behavior requires remediation before publication.

high_findings.title: Shell Command Execution in Server Helper
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The helper accepts --server values from command-line input and passes each server command to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This enables shell metacharacter execution if an untrusted prompt, project file, or user-provided value is used to construct the server command. The later subprocess.run call also executes an arbitrary trailing command, which is expected for the helper but increases blast radius when the skill is used automatically.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly combines user-supplied server strings with shell=True, which is a well-known command injection pattern. The helper is intended for local execution, but that does not remove the injection risk.

high_findings.title: Instruction Discourages Source Inspection Before Script Execution
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill tells agents not to read bundled script source before running the script except in limited cases. This is dangerous marketplace guidance because the bundled helper performs command execution, and discouraging review can hide risky behavior from automated agents and users.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text clearly discourages source inspection of bundled scripts before use. It is not a full system-message override, but it is concerning in context because the script executes shell commands.

medium_findings.title: Local Browser Automation Can Touch Localhost and Filesystem Artifacts
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The examples navigate to localhost pages and write logs or screenshots to local output paths. This is normal for web application testing, but users should understand that pages under test may expose local application data in screenshots, console logs, or DOM inspection output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations show legitimate local testing behavior, not exfiltration. The residual concern is data exposure through generated screenshots and logs.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Benign Localhost or License References
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The detected hardcoded URLs are localhost test targets or Apache License URLs. They do not send secrets to an external service, and no evidence found of hidden network exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are visible and ordinary: localhost testing endpoints and Apache license links. There is no code path that transmits credentials to those license URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text-Match False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts occur in license prose, metadata text, or argparse help text rather than cryptographic API usage. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these lines contain ordinary words such as description, designation, or license language. They are not imports or calls to cryptographic primitives.

low_findings.title: Documentation Code Fence Alerts Are Not Ruby Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The SKILL.md external-command alerts come from Markdown inline code and fenced examples, not Ruby backtick operators. The examples still instruct command execution, but the specific Ruby backtick detection is inaccurate.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited file is Markdown documentation and the backtick characters delimit inline code or code blocks. There is no Ruby source file or Ruby execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper starts server commands with shell=True using values parsed from command-line arguments. Replace this with argument-list execution or strict command allowlisting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present and the command string originates from --server input. This is the strongest confirmed security issue in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bundled Script Black-Box Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends treating bundled scripts as black boxes before source review. This is unsafe for marketplace distribution when those scripts execute commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The guidance is explicit and risky in context. It is not confirmed malicious, but it weakens user and agent review of executable helper code.

content.value_statement: Frontend testing often needs real browser checks, screenshots, and console logs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Playwright workflows for local web applications.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of synchronous Python Playwright scripts for local web application testing
Provides a helper script that can start one or more local servers before running automation
Shows how to wait for dynamic pages to reach network idle before inspecting the DOM
Demonstrates screenshot capture for rendered pages and static HTML files
Includes examples for discovering buttons, links, inputs, and console messages
Supports testing localhost URLs and file-based HTML pages through browser automation
limitations: The helper script can execute local commands and should not use untrusted command strings
It does not provide a full test framework, assertion library, or report generator
Examples are templates and usually need selectors, ports, and paths adjusted for each app
It focuses on local browser testing and does not manage remote device farms or cloud browsers

use_cases.title: Verify Local Frontend Changes
use_cases.description: Run a local server, open the app in Chromium, interact with controls, and capture evidence for a UI change.

use_cases.title: Debug Dynamic UI Behavior
use_cases.description: Wait for JavaScript to finish, inspect rendered elements, collect console output, and identify failing selectors.

use_cases.title: Review Static HTML Prototypes
use_cases.description: Open local HTML files with Playwright, exercise form fields, and save before-and-after screenshots.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Screenshot a Local App
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a running development server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the webapp-testing skill to open my app at http://localhost:5173, wait for network idle, and capture a full-page screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Find Interactive Elements
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright to inspect the rendered page, list visible buttons, links, and inputs, then recommend stable selectors for testing.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Local Server and Test Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Start my local development server safely, run a Playwright script for the sign-in flow, capture console logs, and report failures.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose a Complex UI Regression
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced debugging across screenshots, DOM, and logs
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a focused Playwright diagnostic for this UI regression. Compare rendered state, console messages, selectors, and screenshots before suggesting the fix.

output_examples.input: Check whether the dashboard loads at localhost and save evidence.
output_examples.output: The browser opened the local dashboard, waited for network idle, captured a full-page screenshot, and reported the visible navigation items.

output_examples.input: Find why my submit button test fails.
output_examples.output: The inspection found the button text changed after render, identified a more stable role-based selector, and noted one related console warning.

output_examples.input: Exercise this static HTML form and confirm the submitted state.
output_examples.output: The static page opened from a local file, the form fields were filled, the submit action completed, and screenshots were saved before and after submission.

best_practices: Review bundled scripts before execution and avoid passing untrusted command strings to server helpers
Wait for network idle or a specific selector before inspecting dynamic application state
Use stable selectors such as roles, labels, IDs, or intentional test attributes where available
anti_patterns: Do not run server commands copied from untrusted pages, logs, or model output without review
Do not inspect a dynamic app before JavaScript has rendered the target state
Do not rely on brittle selectors that only match temporary layout text or hidden elements

faq.question: Does this skill run real browser automation?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides Python Playwright scripts that launch Chromium, open pages, interact with elements, and capture screenshots or logs.

faq.question: Can it start my development server?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a helper for local server lifecycle management, but command strings must be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Is this safe for untrusted applications?
faq.answer: Use caution. Browser automation can execute application JavaScript and save page data in screenshots or console logs.

faq.question: Does it support static HTML files?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include file-based Playwright navigation for local HTML prototypes.

faq.question: Does it replace a full QA test suite?
faq.answer: No. It provides automation patterns and examples, not a complete framework with fixtures, reports, and assertions.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
